coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as singlet oxygen producer or radical quencher - The matter of functionalization with urea/thiourea

coredata.dc:description: Due to their low cost and possible green synthesis, high stability and resistance to photobleaching, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be considered as one of the class of carbon nanomaterials which may have great potential as an agent for photosensitized oxygen activation. In such a way, GQDs can be used as a theranostic agent in photodynamic therapy. In this work pristine GQDs, GQDs irradiated with gamma rays and GQDs doped with N and N, S atoms are produced using a simple, green approach. By using different techniques (AFM, HR-TEM, SEM-EDS, FTIR, XRD, PL and UV–Vis) we investigated structural and optical properties of the new types of GQDs. We showed that GQDs functionalized with thiourea (GQDs-TU) completely lost the ability to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) upon photoexcitation while functionalization with urea (GQDs-U) improves the capability of GQDs to produce 1O2 upon the same conditions. Thus, presented GQDs modification with urea seems like a promising approach for the production of the efficient photosensitizer. On the opposite, GQDs-TU are efficient OH quencher. Due to high singlet oxygen production and low cytotoxicity below 100 μg/mL against HeLa cells, GQDs-U is a good candidate as an agent in photodynamic therapy at this concentration.

TEM and HR-TEM micrographs of GQDs (a, b) and GQDs-U (c, d), respectively.
SEM images and EDS spectra of GQD-U (a), GQD-50-U (d), GQDs-TU (c) and GQDs-50-TU (d).

XRD diffraction pattern of the modified GQDs: GQD-50-U, GQD-U, GQD-50-TU, and GQD-TU, as indicated in the image.

EPR spectra with TEMP as a spin trap recorded after 1 (a), 7 (b) and 15 min (c) of UV irradiation. Comparison of relative intensities of EPR signals for each sample (d).

In panels a-c images were taken at 10× objective. In panels d and e images were taken at 20× objective. Scale bar is 100 μm.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their low cost and possible green synthesis, high stability and resistance to photobleaching, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be considered as one of the class of carbon nanomaterials which may have great potential as an agent for photosensitized oxygen activation. In such a way, GQDs can be used as a theranostic agent in photodynamic therapy. In this work pristine GQDs, GQDs irradiated with gamma rays and GQDs doped with N and N, S atoms are produced using a simple, green approach. By using different techniques (AFM, HR-TEM, SEM-EDS, FTIR, XRD, PL and UV–Vis) we investigated structural and optical properties of the new types of GQDs. We showed that GQDs functionalized with thiourea (GQDs-TU) completely lost the ability to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) upon photoexcitation while functionalization with urea (GQDs-U) improves the capability of GQDs to produce 1O2 upon the same conditions. Thus, presented GQDs modification with urea seems like a promising approach for the production of the efficient photosensitizer. On the opposite, GQDs-TU are efficient OH quencher. Due to high singlet oxygen production and low cytotoxicity below 100 μg/mL against HeLa cells, GQDs-U is a good candidate as an agent in photodynamic therapy at this concentration.

ce:section.ce:para: With a lateral size below 100 nm and a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be considered as very small fragments of graphene oxide [1–3]. Considering the small diameter and the height of <10 graphene layers, GQDs are categorized as a 0-Dimensional nanomaterial [4]. Due to this structure, GQDs are highly soluble in water and polar organic solvents. Owing to the small size, graphene core and functional groups, GQDs are photoluminescent material which makes them interesting for application in bioimaging [5–7]. The origin of photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs is still subject of scientific debates but it is most frequently explained by the size of π-domains (quantum confinement effect) and surface states (the edge effect) of quantum dots [8,9].

Apart from PL, GQDs show numerous interesting properties such as the low toxicity, high chemical stability as well as long-term resistivity to photobleaching [10–12]. Thanks to these characteristics, GQDs have attracted considerable scientific attention in many different fields: sensing, bioimaging, photodynamic therapy, catalysis, etc. [13–17]

Photodynamic therapy is the treatment of both carcinoma and bacterial infections and it is based on photo-excited production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of both molecular oxygen and photosensitizer, a compound able to absorb the energy upon light exposure and to efficiently transfer the energy toward the molecule of oxygen [17,18].These events lead to the formation of ROS which caused the toxic effects on both tumor tissue and bacterial cells [19]. As good photosensitizer can be considered compounds or materials which are water-soluble, resistive to photobleaching, non-toxic in the absence of light, an efficient producer of ROS during light exposure, etc. GQDs can fulfill most of these prerequisites and attracts the interest of the scientists as a potential photosensitizer [20–22].

Aside from the modification of GQD size or their level of oxidation (reduction or defects), the properties of dots can be tailored by chemical doping with different add-atoms as well as the level of doping [14,15,23].The introduction of heteroatoms into sp2 domains of the GQD core can cause disorientation of the electron network. The charge distribution, as well as the spin states of doped domains, is regulated by this network [24]. After doping GQDs with nitrogen in 2012 [25], many different atoms have been introduced in the doping procedures, such as sulfur, phosphorus, boron, potassium, selenium, sodium, fluorine and chlorine [16,23,26–30].These doping of GQDs can affect their electrical, optical and chemical properties.

The most common doped GQDs are N doped ones [13,31–33].Considering similar atomic radius (for C is 0.77 and for N is 0.70 Å) and larger electronegativity (for C is 2.55 and for N is 3.04), N atom is easiest to incorporate into carbon network. In this network, N can form ether graphitic N, pyrollic or pyridinic bonds. These dots are mostly studied for their application in catalysis, as a probe in photoluminescence or as electrode material in batteries [15]. Nitrogen atoms are usually incorporated into graphene during synthesis. Also doping with dual heteroatoms was used to modulate the properties of GQDs. Thus S, N co-doped GQDs were usually prepared in the bottom-up procedure and used in detection and photocatalysis [34–36].

Considering our previous studies [18,21,37], GQDs showed a promising behavior as an agent in photodynamic therapy. In this study, we first synthesize GQDs using the electrochemical procedure [38] and modulate the structure of GQDs by using thermal treatment with urea and thiourea in the duration of just 5 min. This simple, fast and low-cost procedure was employed in order to obtain N- and N, S- co-doped GQDs. By introducing different heteroatoms in the known GQDs structure, we try to improve the efficiency of GQDs to produce singlet oxygen upon photoexcitation. Also, by introducing heteroatoms (N, S) in already pre-synthesized GQDs we are able to analyze solely the effect of heteroatoms on photodynamic behavior. Considering that gamma irradiation on GQDs was already proven as an effective treatment to increase the ability of GQDs to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon illumination [18], we also modified gamma-irradiated GQDs with urea and thiourea and investigated the structure, morphology, and the ability to produce ROS. In order to understand the photochemistry of synthesized samples, we also investigate the ability of all produced GQDs to quench radical species.

GQDs have been synthesized using the previously described procedure [21,38]. As a starting material, we used the spectroscopic graphite electrodes which were bought from Ringsdorff Werke GmbH (Bonn, Germany), 99.999% purity (ø = 3.05 mm). The electrodes were washed thoroughly before synthesis with MiliQ water and ethanol. In order to modify their structure, we used gamma irradiation, in a dose of 50 kGy where the medium for irradiation was MiliQ water with isopropyl alcohol [18]. Then both, nonirradiated (in the further text pristine or p-GQDs) and irradiated GQDs were used for functionalization with urea and thiourea. We used 50 mg GQDs and 1 g of urea/thiourea and mixed these powders in a mortar. The reactions with both urea and thiourea with GQDs are presented in Scheme 1 . Then the mixture was placed in a flask and heated (with a heat gun) for 5 min. The final mixtures were diluted in water and filtered, followed by washing with water and methanol to remove free urea/thiourea. Obtained samples were named GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U for nonirradiated and irradiated GQDs treated with urea, respectively. Samples of GQDs functionalized with thiourea named GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed using Quesant (Agoura Hills, CA) microscope operating in tapping mode in the air, at room temperature. We used AFM Q-WM300 probe, rotated, monolithic silicon probe for non-contact high-frequency applications. Standard silicon tips (Nano And More Gmbh, Wetzlar, Germany) were used, with a force constant of 40 N/m. For the preparation of sample surfaces, GQDs were dispersed in MiliQ water in a concentration of 0.25 mg/mL. Dispersions were spin-coated on mica which was used as the substrate. For AFM images analysis, Gwyddion software was used [39].

High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) JEOL JEM-2100F was used to analyze GQDs structure and morphology. Powder samples of GQDs were dispersed in water in a concentration of 1 mg/mL and deposited on carbon laced copper grids. The lateral size of GQDs and interlayer distance was measured from HRTEM images using ImageJ software.

The morphology of the sample was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6390LV) in vacuum at room temperature, with an acceleration voltage of 3 keV. The elemental composition of samples was analyzed with energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS, Oxford Aztec X-max).

Fourier Transform-InfraRed (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode on the Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FTIR instrument. The spectral resolution was 2 cm−1. GQD in the form of powders were used for FTIR measurements.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured on the Rigaku Smart Lab diffractometer operating with Cu Kα1,2 radiation at 30 mA and 40 kV at room temperature. Diffraction data were recorded in a 2θ range of 5 to 60° and counting time of 0.7° per min in 0.02° steps.

Absorption measurements were performed at Spectrophotometer Shimadzu UV–Visible UV-2600 (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) in the ranges of wavelengths from 200 to 800 nm with 1 nm step. The GQD powders were dispersed in MiliQ water in a concentration of 0.25 mg/mL and recorded at 20 °C, in the air environment.

UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Evolution 600 UV/Vis spectrophotometer, equipped with DRA-EV-600 Diffuse Reflectance Accessory. The diffuse reflectance was recorded over the range of 300 to 800 nm at 20 °C, in a powder sample.

Photoluminescent (PL) spectra were obtained using Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrometer. The samples of GQDs were dispersed in methanol, in the concentration of 0.25 mg/mL and PL spectra were measured under excitation wavelengths of 300, 320, 340, 360, 380 and 400 nm. The integration time was 0.1 s. The spectra were collected in the air environment, at a temperature of 25 °C in the quartz cuvette with 1 cm path length and 4 mL volume.

Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) analyses were performed using an EPR Spectrometer Mini Scope 300, Magnettech, Berlin, Germany, operating at a nominal frequency of 9.5 GHz. The microwave power was 0.32 mW (microwave attenuation of 25 dB), with a modulation amplitude of 0.2 mT. A progressive saturation study was performed. Power saturation curve (PSC) shows a maximum between 1 and 10 mW. Below 1 mW the PSC was close to linearity. We estimated that the most acceptable ratio of PSC linearity and S/N ratio is at 0.32 mW (microwave attenuation 25 dB).

The production of 1O2 in aerated GQD dispersions was measured by EPR using a 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as a spin trap agent. This method is based on the reaction between molecules of TEMP and singlet oxygen, which form a stable radical adduct, (2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidin-1-yl)oxyl (TEMP-1O2 or TEMPO). This product has a characteristic EPR triplet signal. For EPR-spin trap analysis, GQD samples were dissolved in air-saturated ethanol in a concentration of 0.2 wt% and TEMP was added (at a final concentration of 30 mM). These mixtures were exposed to UV–Vis light (λ > 300 nm) for 15 min. EPR was measured before and during light exposure. In order to calculate the concentration of produced 1O2, we recorder EPR spectrum of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) solution in ethanol as standard. The concentration of DPPH was 84 μM. We choose DPPH solution as a standard due to the fact that this molecule is a stable free radical meaning that the integrated intensity of EPR spectrum is proportional to free radical concentration [18]. Thus, this we calculated the integrated intensity of each EPR spectra of TEMPO produced by different samples and divided by the value obtained for DPPH solution. Due to the fact that the concentration of TEMPO is directly proportional to the concentration of 1O2 and that values of TEMPO concentration are normalized on the value of DPPH concentration, the ration between singlet oxygen concentration as a function of UV illumination time was established.

Investigation of radical scavenging activity (RSA) was studied using DPPH free radical assay [40]. The molecule of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) is a stable radical species with strong absorption in the UV–Vis spectrum centered at 518 nm. When reacting with antioxidants, DPPH changes the color from deep violet to yellow and this can be easily followed by UV Vis spectroscopy. For investigation of RSA of GQDs, we prepared the fresh solution of DPPH in concentration of 100 μM in methanol and added GQD samples in different concentrations, from 0 to 200 μg/mL, incubated the mixtures for 1 h in dark and after measured the intensity of absorbance at Spectrophotometer Shimadzu UV–Visible UV-2600 (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). As a standard, we used a methanol solution of ascorbic acid in the same concentration range as GQDs. The RSA of GQDs was calculated using the formula RSA(%) = (Ac – AGQDs) / Ac × 100, where Ac is the intensity of absorption of control (DPPH in methanol) and AGQD is the intensity of the absorption band of a mixture of GQDs with DPPH also in methanol. Ascorbic acid was used as a standard. Measurements were replicated three times.

The ability of GQDs to quench hydroxyl radicals was investigated using rhodamine B (RHB) as a colorimetric target molecule. Hydroxyl radicals are generated by UV decomposition of hydrogen peroxide. In each vial, 0.02 mM of RHB and 0.03 M H2O2 was mixed with GQD samples in different concentration (from 10 to 500 μg/mL) and irradiated under UV lamp wavelength of 300 nm for 1 h. After illumination, the mixtures were recorded on Spectrophotometer Shimadzu UV–Visible UV-2600. The intensity of absorbance at 554 nm was measured and used to calculate OH radical scavenging activity using formula: RSA (%) = (AGQDs / AC) × 100, where AGQDs is the intensity of the absorbance at 554 nm of mixture RHB, H2O2 mixed with GQD samples and AC is the intensity of the absorbance of RHB solution without H2O2 and GQDs.

In vitro cytotoxicity against HeLa cells was also conducted. HeLa cells (ATCC® CCL-2™) were grown in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) with low glucose supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, MEM Non-Essential Amino Acids and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (all Gibco). Cells were kept at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2.

For MTT assay HeLa and MRC-5 cells were seeded at concentration 5 × 104 cells/mL in 96-weel plates and allowed to attach overnight. Cells were treated with increasing concentrations of GQDs and GQDs-U (1, 10, 50, 100, 200 and 300 μg/mL) for three-time intervals 24, 48 and 72 h. At the indicated time points medium was removed, cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline and MTT (in concentration 5 mg/mL) (Merck) was added to each well. After further 4 h incubation MTT solution was replaced with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Absorbance was measured at 550 nm on a microplate reader (TECAN). Cell viability was expressed as the percentage of absorbance relative to vehicle control (HeLa cells treated with methanol). Experiments were performed in triplicates, with at least six replicate wells for each sample per assay.

Fluorescence imaging was investigated using HeLa cells. They were seeded on cover slips in 12 well plate and cultured 24 h at 37 °C. Aqueous solutions of p-GQDs and GQDs-U were mixed with cell culture medium and add to the cells in the final concentration of 200 μg/mL. After 48 h of incubation medium was removed, cells were washed three times in PBS and fresh medium was added during optical imaging. Cellular uptake of p-GQD and GQDs-U were examined using Olympus BX51 fluorescence microscope with DAPI filter. Images were captured at 10× and 20× objective and analyzed using Cytovision 3.1 software (Applied Imaging Corporation, USA).

Stability and dispersibility of GQD in different biological media were investigated: in saline, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), the mixture of medium and 10% of fetal bovine serum and 100% of the same serum. Samples of GQDs were added to selected biological media at the 300 μg/mL concentration of GQDs which was the highest applied concentration for the study of cytotoxicity. The stability of GQDs was investigated using Quesant AFM microscope.

In this procedure, we used an already existing procedure for other carbon nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes) as a simple and effective process to overcome solubility and side reactions problems from the water [41,42]. At the melting point of urea (133 °C) and thiourea (174 °C), the yellowish powder instantly became dark yellow or brown respectively. Ford et al. mixed carbon nanotubes with urea in the mass ratio 1:40 [41,42], while we used lower mass ratio, 1:20 for both urea and thiourea. After the reaction mixture was cooled down to room temperature, water was added (500 mL) and filtered through a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) (0.45 μm) membrane. The solid material re-dispersed in water and filtered again. The procedure repeated 4 times (the excess of urea and thiourea were completely removed). As a first point on the characterization of the materials, we checked the morphology. The changes in the morphology of GQDs were followed by AFM and obtained images were analyzed using Gwyddion software. The representative images are shown in Figs. 1 and 2
. AFM images show that after modification with urea, the GQDs are homogeneous, well-dispersed and spherical-shaped, while few agglomerates can be observed in the GQDs-TU sample (Fig. 1c and d). Modification of gamma-irradiated GQDs with urea as well as thiourea did not cause significant morphological changes (Fig. 2b–e), considering that GQDs preserved their shape and the size. The following average heights were obtained: 1.14 (for p-GQDs), 1.26 (for GQDs-U), 1.29 (for GQDs-TU), 2.00 (for GQDs-50), 1.41 (GQDs-50-U) and 1.66 nm (GQDs-50-TU). The functionalization of p-GQDs with both urea and thiourea caused an increase in the height of GQDs. Considering a relatively low increase in GQDs height, the addition of the new functional groups in the structure of dots is the reason for described morphological changes.

On the other hand, the height of gamma-irradiated GQDs was reduced upon functionalization with both urea and thiourea. We previously observed that gamma irradiation causes the changes in the number of graphene layers, from monolayers noticed for pristine GQDs up to three graphene layers observed in GQDs, irradiated at 20 kGy [18]. The lowering of GQD height after functionalization could be the result of cleavage of interlayer bonds formed upon irradiation and yielding to intercalation between the layers.

AFM analysis of functionalized GQDs showed that after functionalization of GQD with both urea and thiourea, dots preserved their dispersibility and shape. The changes were noted only in the height: the increase for GQDs-U and GQDs-TU and lowering the height of GQDs-50-U and GQDs-50-TU. These changes are explained by the formation of new functional groups and the breaking of the interlayer bonds, respectively.

We also used HR-TEM to study the structure and morphology of GQDs. In Fig. 3a and c, TEM micrographs of GQDs and GQDs-U have presented as well as HR-TEM images of the same samples (b and d). The spherical particles are detected in both samples as well as a relatively narrow size distribution between 5.8 and 21.1 nm for GQDs and between 7.0 and 17.6 nm for GQDs-U. The average lateral dimension is around 11.5 nm for p-GQDs and 10.9 nm for GQDs-U sample according to the diameter measurement conducted on >50 dots. High-resolution TEM images revealed a high crystallinity of GQDs-U dots with the lattice parameter of around 0.21 nm which stems from (1120) lattice fringe of graphene, which matches with previous reports [43,44]. The value of this parameter is significantly lower compared to 0.33 nm, measured for p-GQDs. This result reveals that the thermal treatment of GQDs with urea induce the improvement of GQD crystallinity.

However, except for the morphology, it is important to understand the chemical changes of the GQDs. In order to investigate the elemental composition of modified GQDs, SEM-EDS analysis was conducted. In the structure of pristine GQDs, atoms of C, O, and H are detected [18,45]. SEM images and appropriate EDS spectra of all samples are displayed in Fig. 4 . Apart from carbon and oxygen which are detected in all samples, in the GQDs modified with urea, atoms of nitrogen are also recorded. In the GQD-TU and GQDs-50-TU samples, atoms of sulfur are identified as well.

Furthermore, elemental mapping was performed on these samples and the results of these measurements are shown in Fig. 5 . SEM images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) maps of the GQDs-50-U and GQDs-50-TU are presented. Individual elemental distribution of each detected element is presented also (pink for carbon, yellow for oxygen, blue for nitrogen and green for sulfur). As can be observed all detected elements are equally distributed over the analyzed surface, which was 40 × 40 μm2.

The aforementioned results showed that upon modification of GQDs, the elemental composition of dots is changed: modification with the urea-induced incorporation of N atoms in GQD structure, while with thiourea both S and N were detected in GQDs. It was observed the following percentage of heteroatoms: 74.06% of O and 25.94% of N for GQDs-50-U, while 71.17% of O, 20.61% of N, 8.22% of S for GQDs-50-TU. EDS mapping proved that the modified GQDs are homogenous in the sense of chemical composition.
Further evidence to the nature of incorporated both S and N atoms in GQDs, is given by FTIR spectroscopy.

Firstly, the functional groups of pristine and gamma-irradiated GQDs were identified (Fig. 6a and b). The FT-IR spectrum of p-GQDs (Fig. 6a) shows a strong band at 763 cm−1 which stems from the aromatic CH bending vibrations, while bands at around 1570 and 3000 cm−1 are the result of the CC stretching carbon atoms in the aromatic ring [18,46].These bands are working as an indication for sp2-hybridized C atoms in the structure of GQDs. The bands which are associated with CO vibration of alkoxy groups are located at 1045 and 2974 cm−1, while at 1010 and 1365 cm−1 are located bands from the vibration of CO bonds and symmetric stretching of COOH groups. Also, the band at 1730 cm−1 is due to the vibration of CO bonds in carboxyl functional groups. The band at around 2700 is due to vibrations of bonds in aromatic aldehyde, while at 2780 and 2933 cm−1 are bands which stem from CH and CH2 groups [18]. The broadband around 3400 cm−1 is due to the vibration of OH bonds in water or in COH functional groups.

In the FTIR spectrum of gamma-irradiated GQDs, the bands are similar as in the spectrum of pristine GQDs. These measurements showed that gamma-irradiated GQDs have polar functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxylic, and hydroxyl [18]. But also, the π-conjugated core was identified in FTIR spectra (bands at 763, 1570 and 3000 cm−1) which prove that aromatic domains of GQDs are survived gamma irradiation.

On the other hand, spectra of both GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U are significantly different from both pristine and gamma-irradiated GQDs. As can be observed in the FTIR spectrum of GQDs-U, the new band at around 3100 cm−1 is assigned to NH stretching vibration of amine groups [47].Also, the additional band at 1260 cm−1 which stems from the stretching vibration of the amide bond (CN bonds) were observed [48]. The band at 1730 cm−1 which is the characteristic for CO stretching vibration of the carboxylic group almost disappeared and a new band at 1650 cm−1 corresponding to the CO characteristic stretching band of the amide group appeared [48,49]. The band at 1090 cm−1 also appeared and it is assigned to CN bonds [47,50]. Apart from N containing bonds, in the spectrum of GQDs-U, new O-related bands are observed: at 885 and 1170 cm−1 which stem from CO vibration in epoxy functional groups. The appearance of the bands which stem from NH and CN bonds (3100, 1260, 1650 and 1090 cm−1 respectively) indicate the successful introduction of the nitrogen atom in the structure of GQDs. Furthermore, additional O-functional groups were detected, such as epoxy groups. One more change is observed: the band at 1365 cm−1 completely disappeared after functionalization with urea probably due to the conversion of carboxyl groups into amide bonds.

In the FTIR spectrum of GQDs-50-U, the same bands but with a higher intensity were observed: around 3100 cm−1 is the band which is assigned to amine groups, at 1260 cm−1to amide bond (CN bonds), at 1730 cm−1 stems to CO stretching vibration of the carboxylic group, at 1650 cm−1 corresponding to the CO bonds in the amide group, at 1090 cm−1 is assigned to CN bonds while at 885 and 1170 cm−1 are bands from epoxy functional groups. In the FTIR spectrum of GQDs-50-U, the additional band was observed at 2310 cm−1. This band was ascribed to the isocyanate group (NCO) [51].

The intensity of all bands in FTIR spectra of GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU is much lower compared to spectra of p-GQDs and GQD-50, as well as bands in the GQD-U spectra. The new band was noticed at FTIR spectra of GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU centered at 1167 cm−1 and it is assigned to asymmetric stretching vibrations of CS bonds [26]. Another additional band at 2056 cm−1 was noticed in GQDs-50-TU at it is ascribed to stretching vibration of isothiocyanates NCS bonds [52]. Bands at 2692 and 2789 completely disappeared while shifts in bands are observed for the band at 1575, which shifted to 1600, and for peak 1356 shifted to 1371 for GQD-TU and at 1342 for GQDs-50-TU.

These results prove that the modification of GQDs with urea caused structural changes in both pristine and gamma-irradiated GQDs. Most prominent effects are the introduction of amino and amide functional groups as well as the formation of new epoxy bonds. As for functionalization with thiourea, lowering the amount of oxygen-containing functional groups can be observed. Also, the formation of S functional groups was observed in both GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU samples.

The effects of both urea and thiourea on the GQD structure were also studied by XRD (Fig. 7 ). Diffraction peak at 27.7° was observed in spectra of both GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U and it is typical for graphite and corresponds to the (002) plane [53]. The observed peak is a low-intensity, broad peak which indicates significant oxidation of graphite structures and it corresponds to an interlayer distance of 3.21 Å. As for the graphite electrode used for GQDs synthesis, only a strong peak at 26.56° was observed in the XRD pattern [46]. This peak is related to d-spacing of 0.336 nm and the calculated value is typical for interlayer spacing between graphene sheets. In the case of GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U, the peak at 10.6° is observed and it stems from (001) plane characteristic for graphene oxide [54]. Thus, these XRD patterns (Fig. 7, GQDs-50-U and GQDs-U) prove that oxygen-containing functional groups are present in GQDs functionalized with urea.

The broadening of the diffraction line which corresponds to the (002) plane toward lower angles was observed in the spectra of GQDs functionalized with thiourea (Fig. 7, GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU). This peak in spectra of GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU samples was reordered as a broad band centered at 2θ = 27.4° and corresponds to d-spacing of 3.26 Å. These values are lower compared to other reports, where d-spacing of GQDs is around 3.60 Å [55]. A mild lowering in the interlayer spacing could be explained by the chemical reduction of GQDs with both urea and thiourea and is caused by the removing of covalent functional groups from graphene planes in dots structure. A higher d-spacing in GQDs functionalized with thiourea than for GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U suggests that urea cause more efficient removing of covalent functional groups from the graphene structure of GQDs than thiourea, upon the same experimental condition. The average crystallite size was calculated for each sample and we obtained the following values: 8.5 nm for GQD-U, 6.1 nm for GQD-50-U, 1.4 nm for both GQD-TU and GQD-50-TU samples, which is in agreement with the previous assumption that urea causes more efficient reduction of GQDs. For the graphite electrode, we previously obtained that the average crystallite size was 20 nm [46]. A lower crystallite size obtained for GQD sample is due to the presence of functional groups, lower particle size and increased disorder compared to graphite precursor.

Additionally, the appearance of a broad contribution at lower diffraction angles (around 10.6° for GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U) is assigned to the (101) diffraction plane [56,57], which suggests a partial reduction of GQDs functionalized with urea. Also, diffraction patterns of GQDs functionalized with urea display the peak at 24.7°. This peak stems from the (101) diffraction plane of urea (ICDD 00-031-1979).

Optical properties of GQD samples were also affected by the functionalization procedures and the direct proof for these changes was studied by UV Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 8 ). Firstly, we were analyzed the p-GQDs in comparison with the GQDs-U and GQD-50-U and these results are presented in Fig. 8a. As can be observed, GQDs showed a prominent band located at around 218 nm which corresponds to π-π⁎ transitions of aromatic domains of CC bonds. The treatment with urea caused significant changes in the UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 8a), red curve). The main absorption band is shifted from 218 to 231 nm with a significant appear of 2 new bands: one prominent high-intensity band at 290 nm and broad, low-intensity band centered at 397 nm. The position of the absorption band is strongly affected by the synthetic approach as well as the content of oxygen-containing functional groups. These changes in our case are due to the difference in the number of oxygen groups, due to the heat-induced dehydration and decarboxylation [18]. Ma et al. suggested that the restoration of sp2 domains upon chemical reduction of graphene oxide leads to a shift in the main absorption band toward the higher wavelength [58]. Thus, the shift in the main absorption band is related to the chemical reduction of GQDs and the restoration of π conjugated domains [58]. The additional band at 290 nm may be due to the extended π-electron delocalization over the introduction of N-containing functional groups, while the third band can be assigned to n-π* transitions of CO bonds [59].

Surprisingly, the functionalization of gamma-irradiated GQDs with urea cause only a small shift in the absorption band, at 221 nm (green curve in Fig. 8a) which suggests a lower level of chemical reduction. This sample was actually exposed to the two steps of the reduction: first gamma irradiation in isopropyl alcohol/water medium [18] and the second doping and heating with urea.

The main absorption band is also shifted to 223 nm for both GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU samples (Fig. 8b). An additional shoulder is observed in both spectra, at around 242 nm. The maximum of absorption of thiourea as appeared around 241 nm [60], thereby the shoulder band can be assigned to the electronic transition in thiourea molecular orbital. Also, a wide, low-intensity band at around 350 nm which was also noticed by Jin et al. [61], as well as an increase in the absorption intensity at higher wavelength can be observed.

These results suggest that the heat treatment with urea caused the restoration of π conjugated domains. On the other hand, the functionalization of GQDs with thiourea presents low effect on oxygen functional groups.

In parallel with the optical properties, we examined the electronic properties of GQDs by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. The energy band gap of GQDs was calculated from the obtained reflectance spectra using the Kubelka−Munk function (F(R) = (1 − R)2 / 2R) (Table 1 ) [62]. The lowering of the bandgap can be observed for both the functionalization procedures (urea and thiourea). More specifically, from 2.17 (for GQDs) and 2.13 (GQDs-50) to 1.80 eV (1.81 eV for GQDs-50-U) was observed for both pristine and irradiated GQDs after functionalization with urea. Further lowering in the value of Eg was observed upon the modification of GQDs with thiourea. The value of Eg is affected by the fraction of sp2 C atoms and O-containing functional groups. The narrowing of the bandgap can be due to the contributions of doping atoms in the lattice [63], the enlargement of the π conjugated sp2-carbon domains or/and due to functionalization of GQDs with electron-donating chemical groups as a way to introduce an intermediate n-orbital between π and π* orbitals [64]. As for GQDs-TU and GQDs-50-TU, reduced Eg values are due to higher πelectron cloud density due to electron-donating effects of thiourea [65]. In the case of GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U, the narrowed bandgap could be explained by chemical reduction which induced an increase in the π conjugated domains.

For confirming the changes in their optoelectronic properties we run PL spectra of GQDs (Fig. 9 ). As can it be observed, all modified GQDs preserved PL properties even after functionalization except for the case of GQDs-50-U (Fig. 9). All samples showed excitation-dependent emission, which is one of the characteristics of the GQDs materials, due to the size distribution of dots [66].Functionalized GQDs possess the lower photoluminescence compared to starting material (p-GQDs), as can be observed in Fig. 9f. In addition, the excitation wavelength at which GQDs showed the highest intensity of emission was shifted: for p-GQDs was at 340 nm, GQDs-U and GQDs-50-U at 400 nm, and for both sample of GQDs which are modified with thiourea at 380 nm. It is established that the PL of GQDs depends on both graphene core and the surrounding functional groups [8,9].Considering the edge groups, for top-down synthesized GQDs, carboxyl and amide functional groups are primarily responsible for the green emission and the hydroxyl groups are responsible for the blue emission while epoxy groups have the ability to induce non-radiative recombination. As a consequence, the chemical composition of GQDs has a key role in the mechanism of PL. [8,9]

Considering the aforementioned results of chemical analysis of p- and modified GQDs, we can conclude that the reduced PL intensity in the case of GQDs functionalized with urea is due to the formation of epoxy groups which cause non-radiative recombination. It is known that the urea at the melting temperature decomposes to ammonia and isocyanic acid, which can give reactions with carboxy or hydroxy group to yield to amides and upon excess can generate ureido groups (OCONH2) [42]. In the case of functionalization with thiourea, in the structure of GQDs were identified less electronegative S groups (>CS and NCS) but epoxy groups were not identified. Thus, the density of electrons in GQDs-TU is enhanced which is yielded in higher PL intensity compared to GQDs functionalized with urea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EPR: production of singlet oxygen upon UV illumination
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the ability of modified GQDs to produce singlet oxygen upon light irradiation and to investigate the possibility of its use in photodynamic therapy as a photosensitizer, we use spin trap-EPR spectroscopy. For the selective detection of singlet oxygen, we used TEMP as a spin trap (Fig. 10 ) [67]. As we can see in Fig. 10a, shortly after light irradiation (only after a minute), the characteristic triplet signal of TEMPO can be observed. After 7 min of UV irradiation, the intensity of the TEMPO signal increased for all the samples. The smallest increase in TEMPO signal was noticed for both GQDs functionalized with thiourea, GQDs-TU, and GQDs-50-TU. After 15 min of irradiation, the highest intensity of TEMPO signal was observed for the GQDs-50 and GQDs-U samples. Our previous study showed that gamma irradiation of GQDs at a dose of 50 kGy significantly changes the ability of GQDs to produce singlet oxygen by increasing the concentration of produced 1O2 [18]. Herein, we observed that functionalization of GQDs with urea also improves the capability of GQDs to produce singlet oxygen upon light irradiation.

In Fig. 10d, the concentration of singlet oxygen as a function of illumination time is presented. Both GQDs which were functionalized with urea show a significant singlet oxygen production, similar to the production of GQDs-50 (Fig. 10d). Under same experimental conditions, GQDs showed different photochemical behavior: the concentration of singlet oxygen increased up to maximum (which is reached after 6 to 8 min) for GQDs, GQDs-50, and GQDs-50-U while in the case of GQDs-U the concentration of 1O2 continually increased during the whole measuring time. Different trends in singlet oxygen production suggest variations in the mechanism of photochemical production of 1O2.

Multistage mechanism of singlet oxygen production was proposed for GQDs by Ge et al. [68] Our study showed that gamma irradiation increases the production of singlet oxygen from illuminated GQDs according to the same mechanism [18]. Herein we observed that also the introduction of N heteroatoms in GQDs structure have a positive effect on photochemical behavior of GQDs while both N and S atoms blocked the ability of GQDs to transfer the energy from excited form of GQDs to molecular oxygen.

Free radical scavenging activity of GQDs functionalized with urea and thiourea was also studied. For measuring of radical scavenging activity, we used 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH) as a model radical. It is a chemically stable radical with a maximum of the absorbance at around 520 nm. In the presence of antioxidants, purple DPPH• radical is quenched and forms a yellow colored DPPH-H complex. After 1 h of incubation of DPPH• with GQDs in the darkness, the intensity of the absorption band at 520 nm was measured in the order to estimate the radical scavenging activity (Fig. 11 ). The spectra of DPPH incubated with GQDs showed that non-modified dots do not show the strong activity to quench radicals (Fig. 11a). On the opposite, GQDs-U reduced the intensity of DPPH absorption band and this is proportional to the concentration of GQDs. The highest activity was observed for a concentration of 200 μg/mL. Then again, in the case of GQDs-TU, only the small changes in the DPPH intensity were observed (Fig. 11c) indicating the low RSA activity. We compared RSA of GQD samples with vitamin C which is a standard antioxidant (Fig. 11d). Significantly high, dose-depended scavenging activity was registered only for GQDs functionalized with urea, while for both p-GQDs and GQDs-TU radical scavenging activity is very low.

We calculated the half-maximal inhibition of the effective concentration (EC50) for GQDs-U and obtained the value of 128.91 ± 4.74 μg/mL. This result suggests the ability of GQDs-U for free radicals quenching and this value is comparable with other reports for GQDs (1269 and 150 μg/mL) [70].

Ruiz et al. suggested that GQDs with OH and amino groups at the edges induce strong hydrogen donating activity and together with extense of π-domains which leads to higher electron delocalization and stabilization [69].

Considering that the OH radicals are the main radicals formed during oxidative stress in a biological environment, we investigated the ability of functionalized GQDs to quench such radicals. The samples of GQDs were incubated for 1 h in the mixture of hydrogen peroxide and RHB under UV lamp. During UV irradiation, hydrogen peroxide decomposes to OH radicals. These radicals cause the change in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of RHB by reducing the intensity of the absorption band at 554 nm. In the presence of OH radical scavenger, the intensity of the RHB absorption band is preserved. For p-GQDs (500 μg/mL, highest concentration) the intensity of the band at 554 nm was also increased, indicating the ability of GQDs to quench OH radicals (Fig. 12a). In the case of GQD-U, the intensity of the band at 554 nm also increases but the value of intensity is lower compared to p-GQDs (Fig. 12b). The highest intensities of the absorption band at 554 nm were observed for GQD-TU (Fig. 11c). The intensity of the band at 554 nm increases with GQDs-TU concentration, but in a concentration of 500 μg/mL, the value of intensity is higher than the referent sample. In Fig. 12d are summarized the results of RSA measurements and shows that GQDs-TU has the highest activity to scavenge OH radicals.

Considering the pristine and functionalized GQDs are of similar dimensions, it can be concluded that these parameters are not crucial for the ability of dots to generate singlet oxygen. Based on the aforementioned results we can conclude that crystallinity, chemical composition and electron density of GQDs plays the most important role in the mechanism of ROS production. Thus, the nature of heteroatoms, their electronegativity as well as the effects of functional groups on the density of π-cloud (electron-donating or withdrawing), affect the singlet oxygen production. It is well known that electronegativity decreases in the following order: O > N > S > C. Thus, in the p-GQDs dominant functional groups are O–containing groups with the highest electronegativity. Meanwhile, GQDs functionalized with urea possess amino, amide, and isocyanates (NCO) groups which are weaker electrophiles [71].Due to the changes in the functional groups, the density of electrons in π-cloud increase in comparison to p-GQDs, resulting in the formation of dots with enhanced ability to produce singlet oxygen. As Chong et al. suggested, GQDs photo-generate 1O2 via energy-transfer and electron-transfer pathways [70].So the increase in the density of electrons in π-cloud could improve 1O2 generation through electron-transfer pathways.

Upon functionalization with thiourea, in the structure of GQDs were identified even less electronegative S groups such as >CS and NCS groups. Unexpectedly, these dots have completely lost their ability to produce ROS upon photoexcitation. This could be related to the ability to quench OH radical. Hydroxyl radical scavenging assay showed that GQD-TU possesses very high efficiency to quench OH radicals, even at very low concentration (10 μg/mL induced 86% of radical scavenging activity) which may be the reason for the absence of a photodynamic effect. Considering that sulfur-rich proteins and compound with sulfur efficiently quench ROS [72–75], we assume that our GQDs-TU interact with radicals' species in a similar way.

The formation of ROS from a photosensitizer is a result that takes place during the photoexcitation with incident photon with energy less than the bandgap. Upon functionalization, with both urea and thiourea, the bandgap was lowered, yielding in an increased photodynamic effect of these GQDs, which is connected directly with the chemical changes of the GQDs (doping and reduction) supporting our previously published results [18].

In order to investigate the use of GQDs as an agent in PDT, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of both GQDs and GQDs-U on the HeLa cells (Fig. 13 ). Considering that GQDs-TU were unable to produce singlet oxygen and thus they are unsuitable for application in PDT, we studied cytotoxicity of GQDs and GQDs-U. All measurements were conducted in dark, to avoid the photocytotoxicity. For MTT assays, HeLa cells were exposed to increasing concentrations of GQDs (from 1 to 300 μg/mL) and analyzed after 24, 48 and 72 h of exposure (Fig. 13).

According to MTT assay, p-GQDs do not show any considerable toxicity toward HeLa cells at concentrations up to 300 μg/mL (Fig. 13a). Also, the extended exposure (up to 72 h) of HeLa cells to GQDs did not induce cytotoxic effects, at any applied concentrations (Fig. 13a). This is a promising result considering that after treatment with GQDs, the time for dot elimination is still unknown.

On the opposite, GQDs-U showed a significant cytotoxic effect at concentrations of 100 μg/mL and higher. 24 h treatment of HeLa cells with 100 μg/mLGQDs-U, decreased cell viability to 53% (Fig. 13b). Extended treatments at this concentration, 48 h, and 72 h, led to a further reduction of cell viability to 25 and 27%, respectively (Fig. 13b). These results indicated that prolonged exposure of HeLa cells toward GQDs-U cause stronger toxic effects on the studied cell line. As expected, 24 h treatments with higher concentrations of GQDs-U, 200 and 300 μg/mL, further decreased cell viability to 22 and 21%, respectively (Fig. 13b). These results showed that modified GQDs possess both time and dose-dependent cytotoxic activity.

In vitro cytotoxicity of both p-GQDs and GQDs-U against normal cells MRC-4 were also investigated and these results are presented in Fig. 13c and d, respectively. These investigations show that there is no significant toxic effect of GQDs toward selected normal cell line.

Presented results showed that GQDs are not toxic toward HeLa cells, in dark conditions at concentrations below 300 μg/mL. After modification with urea, GQDs become a toxic agent: they induced death of HeLa cells at a concentration of 100 μg/mL and higher. Considering their use in photodynamic therapy, the PDT agent should be non-toxic in the absence of light and to efficiently produce reactive oxygen species upon photo-excitation [17]. Thus, based on our results obtained for photo-induced singlet oxygen production and investigation of cytotoxicity, GQDs-U are a good candidate as an agent in photodynamic therapy at concentrations below 100 μg/mL.

In order to investigate in vitro fluorescence property of p- and modified GQDs, we incubated HeLa cells with p-GQDs and GQDs-U and these results are presented in Fig. 14 . Both quantum dots increases the visibility of HeLa cell (Fig. 14b and c) while enlarge images (Fig. 14d and e) indicated better visibility cells incubated with GQDs-U.
Presented results indicated the larger potential of GQDs functionalized with urea for bioimaging compared to p-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of GQDs in biological media
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the stability of p-GQDs and U-GQDs in different biological media, we mixed dots with saline, PBS, medium, a mixture of medium and 10% of serum, as well as with pure serum. The photographies of these mixtures are shown in Fig. 15 . As can be observed optically, after the mixing there are no visible signs of precipitation. The media look clear and transparent. These mixtures were then investigated using AFM.

Large scale and AFM images with high magnification for each mixture are presented in Fig. 16 . Due to presence of salts and proteins in selected media, the large, differently shaped structures can be observed in all large scale AFM images (Fig. 16a, c, e, g, i, k, m, o). Apart from those, very small, disk-like structures with the hight below 3 nm can be identified in each sample at high/magnification images (Fig. 16b, d, f, h, j, l, n, p, r, t). Thus it can be concluded that these are GQDs. These small particles are well dispersed and isolated, which indicated that GQDs are stable in all these media.

By adding GQDs, both p- and U-GQDs in different biological media, we were able to estimate the stability of dots in a diverse environment. These experiments prove that produced material do not undergo aggregation or precipitation in selected biological media.

In this paper, for the first time, the structure of graphene quantum dots is modified using a simple and fast approach which is resulted in N and N, S co-doped dots. By using urea, we obtained graphene quantum dots doped with N atoms, with increased crystallinity, enlarged π-domains and narrowed bandgap. These dots upon photoexcitation produce 50% more singlet oxygen and quench 7 times more free radical species compared to non-modified dots.

On the opposite, N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots are unable to produce singlet oxygen upon photoexcitation, but they showed significant antioxidative activity; they quenched 86% OH at a concentration of 10 μg/mL.

Thus N-doped graphene quantum dots are a promising candidate for application in photodynamic therapy while N, S co-doped dots seems to be a very efficient antioxidative agent. Our future work will be devoted to further study the possibility of use N-doped dots as a photodynamic agent using in vivo conditions. As for N, S co-doped dots, we are going to study their ability to quench other radical species and protective effects in biological systems.
